note_tweet.text:
crypto has been one of the greatest “left curve” assets of our generation 

consequently i find it rather ironic that those who have made their fortunes in crypto [via said left-curve thought processes] are now mid-curving their way out of the sector 

i think a lot of the CT timeline is dominated by active perp traders, and it’s been a horrific trading environment on majors primarily due to a lack of volatility; many people have a strategy of chasing breakouts and breakdowns, and given we’ve largely flatlined for the last 2-3 months that strategy will generally have done poorly 

and i think it’s these same guys who think crypto is over, because they conflate their ability to make pnl with crypto prices being able to recover 

it actually feels extremely similar to that 2023 period where we had no vol and people chopped themselves up for ages before just giving up and leaving the market, which was obviously a terrible decision

active traders feel the need to make money at any given moment in time and are generally not good bellwethers for longer-term price action

in the not too distant future i believe i’ll be QTing these tweets when we’re much higher and the same people who’ve left will opportunistically return.

note_tweet.text:
"crypto is dead" 

feels like something i've seen many times now, and the answer is it never really dies

to understand why it doesn't die (and why it always comes back) you have to be a believer in the concept of "price first, narrative second"

the thing about crypto is, it's an absolutely tiny asset class, and it does not take a large amount of capital to pump it...to put that into context the entire market cap of crypto ($2.68T) is below just a single stock, AMZN ($2.85T)

if you believe in the 4y cycle (which i fully do having made the mistake twice in a row), you'll also buy into the belief that a lot of price action is governed by BTC whales...these guys decide to sell when the cycle is over, but they also decide to buy to get it going again

and just when it feels like crypto has nothing going for it, when BTC goes back to 80K, then 90K, then 100K...which it will...suddenly the narrative starts to look sexier and inevitably there will be new fundamental positive factors that we're possibly not even aware of that come to light

i think it's a mistake to call crypto over...the longer it lasts the more likely it is to last forever...and there are big players in the game...way bigger than you or i or anyone dicking around on CT...who can essentially create outcomes should they wish

these thoughts should not be conflated with "everything is immediately going higher right now", maybe there could be further downside (altho my gut says we've seen the lows already), and they often take time to play out (as they have done in past cycles).

but just remember, it only takes a couple of people to start a party, and people like to party.

note_tweet.text:
i think there is a lot of truth to this

pre taking rekt "seriously" i used to tweet about all sorts of things...trading, ai, memecoins etc etc

eventually i realised there was something special behind rekt and the biggest hurdle to achieving it was actually myself

when u look around at the "best" projects...not just in crypto but in the world...their founders/operators literally just bleed the project and nothing else

being on CT is a double-edged sword, because you generally see so many opportunities swing by, but they also serve as temptations and distractions to what it is you're trying to achieve

AI is a big one...can be a proper slippery slope in terms of spending hours on nothingness...but i'm enough of an old man to be self-aware of my vices and stay disciplined to only use it where i think i can specifically benefit @rektdrinks / $REKT, which so far has been great for us internally.

healthmaxxing i think is another one, but the good thing there is we're literally also a CPG company, and we organically have to work on that stuff everyday.

one thing i realised very early on (from the degenz days) is being a project founder is a fkn grind, there are times when its great and you feel euphoric, but the number of hours/days spent in the grind is far far longer...which means to survive the grind you really have to be i) passionate about what you're doing ii) be good at what you're doing iii) enjoy what you're doing....otherwise it is literally impossible to survive the grind periods.

and u definitely want to survive the grind periods because when things get good again, there is no better feeling than seeing your hard work rewarded alongside your community/supporters benefitting.

one thing re grail's comments, i'll never relentlessly "bullpost" - coz i don't want to lead people down the garden path, but i will relentlessly post and produce content thru every day of an bear market like we always have to show how hard we are working, and that we'll never leave.

$REKT

note_tweet.text:
all fair comments.

i feel i've responded with reasonable answers to your questions but of course i can only speak for myself and not the entire landscape of fungible tokens - which is precisely why i think it's important to recognise that it's the underlying project, its team and founders which are the most important factor, rather than if a token is fungible or not.

there are also countless NFT cash grabs, which we don't talk about anymore because they are obviously rugged, just like how there are a lot of good fungible token projects which are acting honestly.

tweets.text:
@DGMD22 all fair comments.

i feel i've responded with reasonable answers to your questions but of course i can only speak for myself and not the entire landscape of fungible tokens - which is precisely why i think it's important to recognise that it's the underlying project, its team

note_tweet.text:
gm. i understand, but as we have discussed here, there are pros and cons to each structure. there is not "one structure fits all", and i think teams should adapt to pursue their goals.

my observation is that you often criticise NFT projects with memecoins because all you wanted to do was "collect NFTs", which is fine, but i also think you have to respect that founders and teams (particularly @LucaNetz who btw didn't create PP, he bought it) may have their own goals and that should be acceptable if they act in good faith.

fortunately the NFT market is big, and if your focus is on collecting NFTs and you believe that strategy tokens are the answer to liquidity, then there are obviously great options there.

i think constantly attacking other projects which have a different setup in an attempt to achieve honest goals is what rubs me the wrong way.

there is not just one route to a journey.

the $REKT weekly is back today at 10am ET https://t.co/pe4FpnJOjD

note_tweet.text:
I don't think it's a flaw, but I think the concept of fungible vs non-fungible tokens serving different purposes is correct.

Ultimately as a collector or trader you can choose what you want to go for, neither is right or wrong, and I think we should stop criticising projects/founders who choose to take a respectful direction.

tweets.text:
@metaboners @DGMD22 I don't think it's a flaw, but I think the concept of fungible vs non-fungible tokens serving different purposes is correct.

Ultimately as a collector or trader you can choose what you want to go for, neither is right or wrong, and I think we should stop criticising

note_tweet.text:
I think the mistake yourself and many others make is that an NFT collection of ~10k images is not scalable if you have ambitions for large growth. 

By definition you can only have a maximum if 10k people in your community. 

Having a token allows you to scale, and I think it’s quite clear that NFT projects which did not launch successful tokens have struggled for mindshare in the last 4 years while NFTs have been “dead”. 

If you are a founder of a company which a clear mission, sitting around for 4 years (or more) waiting for things to get better is not an option. 

It depends on the underlying goal of each project, and provided those goals are honest and genuine, they should all be respected.
